searchDocs.title: Boot
searchDocs.content: On this page Boot This item only works when running on the server. ServerThis item is only intended to be used by the module's authors. Private Show Private Handles initialization of the Admin Abuse system. Creates the AdminAbuse folder in ReplicatedStorage and observes internal state changes to run event Executors. When an event activates, Boot: Spawns the event's Executor function Schedules an automatic expiry thread Registers both with a Trove for clean teardown Sets attributes on the ReplicatedStorage folder so clients can read event state The ReplicatedStorage folder exposes three attributes: EventName — the active event key (or nil) EndTime — unix timestamp when the event expires Duration — the configured event duration in seconds

searchDocs.title: Cache
searchDocs.content: Cache This item only works when running on the server. ServerThis item is only intended to be used by the module's authors. Private A simple deduplication cache used to prevent the same event from being processed more than once. Keyed by the event's endTime timestamp. Both the MessagingService subscription (in AdminAbuse) and the Polling loop check this cache before activating an event. If cache[endTime] is already true, the event is skipped. -- Internal usage: if cache[endTime] then return -- already processed end cache[endTime] = true

searchDocs.title: admin_event​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.admin_event: State Observable State object tracking the currently active event key. Set to nil when no event is running.

searchDocs.title: admin_event_trove​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.admin_event_trove: Trove A Trove used for cleaning up resources associated with the current event. Cleaned automatically whenever the active event changes or expires.

searchDocs.title: admin_event_end_time​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.admin_event_end_time: number Unix timestamp (from os.time()) at which the current event expires. Defaults to 0.

searchDocs.title: admin_event_duration​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.admin_event_duration: number Duration in seconds for new events. Defaults to 60.

searchDocs.title: memstore_update_interval​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.memstore_update_interval: number How often (in seconds) the Polling loop checks MemoryStoreService for cross-server event state. Defaults to 5.

searchDocs.title: messaging_topic​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> InternalState.messaging_topic: string The MessagingService topic name used for cross-server event broadcasts. Defaults to "AA_TOPIC".

searchDocs.title: read​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> Ping.read( channel: string-- The event key to look up ) → number?-- The unix end-time timestamp, or nil Reads the stored end-time for the given event key from MemoryStoreService. Returns nil if the key does not exist or has expired.

searchDocs.title: tap​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> Ping.tap( channel: string-- The event key to write ) → number-- The calculated unix end-time timestamp Writes a new end-time for the given event key to MemoryStoreService and returns it. The end-time is calculated as os.time() + admin_event_duration. The hash map entry is set to expire after admin_event_duration + 60 seconds.

searchDocs.title: admin_event_changed​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> Signals.admin_event_changed: Signal Fires when a new admin event starts executing. The callback receives the event key (string) as its only argument. Exposed publicly via AdminAbuse.onEventChanged.

searchDocs.title: ResourceNodeMultiplier​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> EventMultipliers.ResourceNodeMultiplier: State A State object holding a number that represents an additional multiplier applied to resource node yields. Defaults to 0 (no bonus). Set this inside an event Executor to grant bonus resources during the event, and reset it to 0 in the Trove cleanup.

searchDocs.title: How the Modules Work Together​
searchDocs.content: The package is composed of several internal modules that each handle a specific responsibility. Here's how they fit together:   ┌─────────────────┐ │ AdminAbuse │ ← Public API (init.luau) │ .event() │ │ .onEventChanged│ └────────┬────────┘ │ ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌────────▼────────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐ ┌─────────▼────────┐ │ Ping │ │ Signals │ │ EventMultipliers │ │ MemoryStore │ │ Signal hub │ │ State-based │ │ read/write │ │ │ │ multipliers │ └────────┬────────┘ └─────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ Polling │ ← Polls MemoryStore for cross-server sync └────────┬────────┘ │ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ Boot │ ← Initializes ReplicatedStorage folder │ │ and observes state changes to run executors └────────┬────────┘ │ ┌────────▼────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ InternalState │───────▶│ State │ │ (shared state) │ │ (observable │ └──────────────────┘ │ values) │ │ └──────────────┘ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ Cache │ ← Deduplication cache └──────────────────┘

searchDocs.title: Module Breakdown​
searchDocs.content: AdminAbuse (init.luau)​  The public-facing API. Exposes event() to trigger events and onEventChanged to listen for them. When you call event(), it:  Writes the event end-time to MemoryStoreService via the Ping modulePublishes the event to all servers via MessagingServiceUpdates local InternalState to kick off execution  State​  A lightweight observable value container. Supports :Get(), :Set(), and :Observe(). Used internally by InternalState and EventMultipliers to hold reactive values that trigger callbacks when changed.  InternalState​  The shared state container for the entire package. Holds the current active event, the Trove for cleanup, timing configuration, and the MessagingService topic name. Every internal module reads from this single source of truth.  Boot​  Runs once on startup. Creates an AdminAbuse folder in ReplicatedStorage with attributes (EventName, EndTime, Duration) so the client can read event state. Observes InternalState.admin_event and, when an event activates:  Spawns the event's Executor functionSchedules automatic expiryWires everything into a Trove for clean teardown  Ping​  Interfaces with MemoryStoreService. Provides read() to check an event's end-time and tap() to write a new end-time. This is how event timing persists across servers — even if the originating server shuts down, other servers can discover the event via the memory store.  Polling​  A background loop that runs every few seconds (configurable via InternalState.memstore_update_interval). It reads from MemoryStoreService to discover events that were started on other servers. If it finds an active event this server doesn't know about, it activates it locally. If an event has expired, it cleans up.  Cache​  A simple { [endTime]: true } table that prevents the same event from being processed twice. Both the MessagingService subscription and the Polling loop check this cache before activating an event.  Signals​  Houses the package's Signal instances (built on sleitnick/signal). Currently exposes admin_event_changed, which fires whenever a new event begins executing.  EventMultipliers​  Holds State objects that represent gameplay multipliers tied to admin events. Currently includes ResourceNodeMultiplier — a numeric value that other systems can observe to apply bonus resource yields during events.

searchDocs.title: Cross-Server Flow​
searchDocs.content: Here's what happens end-to-end when you call AdminAbuse.event("SixSeven"):  Ping.tap writes endTime to MemoryStoreServiceMessagingService:PublishAsync broadcasts { Event, EndTime } to all serversThe local server updates InternalState immediatelyBoot's observer fires, spawns the Executor, sets ReplicatedStorage attributes, schedules expiryOther servers receive the MessagingService message, validate it, update their local stateServers that miss the message discover the event via Polling on the next cycleWhen endTime passes, each server's expiry thread (or Polling loop) clears the event and calls Trove:Clean()

searchDocs.title: Installation​
searchDocs.content: All packages are installed via Wally.

searchDocs.title: 1. Configure your Wally registry​
searchDocs.content: Add the Kestrel registry to your wally.toml:  [package] name = "your-name/your-game" version = "0.1.0" registry = "https://github.com/Kestrel-UGC-Solutions/wally-index" realm = "server"

searchDocs.title: 2. Add dependencies​
searchDocs.content: Add the packages you need under [dependencies]:  [dependencies] AdminAbuse = "kestrel/admin-abuse@0.1.0"

searchDocs.title: 3. Install​
searchDocs.content: wally install   Wally will pull the package and its dependencies into your Packages/ directory.

searchDocs.title: Available Packages​
searchDocs.content: Package	Version	DescriptionAdmin Abuse	0.1.0	Cross-server admin event system with automatic synchronization, expiry, and cleanup.

searchDocs.title: Quick Example​
searchDocs.content: local AdminAbuse = require(path.to.AdminAbuse) -- Trigger an event across all servers AdminAbuse.event("SixSeven") -- Listen for event changes AdminAbuse.onEventChanged:Connect(function(eventKey) print("Event started:", eventKey) end)

searchDocs.title: onEventChanged​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> AdminAbuse.onEventChanged: Signal A Signal that fires whenever a new admin event begins executing. The callback receives the event key as its only argument. AdminAbuse.onEventChanged:Connect(function(eventKey: string) print("New event:", eventKey) end)

searchDocs.title: EventMultipliers​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> AdminAbuse.EventMultipliers: EventMultipliers Reference to the EventMultipliers module. Holds observable State objects that represent gameplay multipliers which can be set by event Executors and observed by other game systems. local multiplier = AdminAbuse.EventMultipliers.ResourceNodeMultiplier:Get()

searchDocs.title: Events​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> AdminAbuse.Events: Events Reference to the Events module. Contains the table of registered events keyed by their GlobalEventKey. Each event entry has an Executor function.

searchDocs.title: event​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> AdminAbuse.event( eventKey: string-- The key identifying which event to trigger ) → () Triggers an admin abuse event across all servers. The event is identified by its key from the Events module. When called, this function: Validates the event key and its Executor Persists the event end-time in MemoryStoreService via the Ping module Broadcasts the event to all servers via MessagingService Activates the event locally (re-triggers if the same event is already active) AdminAbuse.event("SixSeven")

searchDocs.title: PollingContext​
searchDocs.content: </> interface PollingContext { hash: MemoryStoreHashMap -- The MemoryStore hash map to poll read: (key: string) → number?-- Function to read an event's end-time pollForKeys: {string}-- Array of event keys to watch } Configuration passed to the Polling loop on startup.

searchDocs.title: new​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> State.new( value: any-- The initial value (can be nil) ) → State Creates a new State object with the given initial value.

searchDocs.title: Set​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> State:Set( newVal: any-- The new value to store ) → () Updates the stored value. If the new value is different from the current value, all registered observer callbacks are invoked with the new value via task.spawn. If the new value is the same as the current value, this is a no-op.

searchDocs.title: Get​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> State:Get() → any Returns the current stored value.

searchDocs.title: Observe​
searchDocs.content: since 0.1.0 </> State:Observe( callback: (value: any) → (),-- Function to call on value change getInitial: boolean?-- If true, fires the callback immediately with the current value ) → () → ()-- Unsubscribe function Registers a callback that fires whenever the value changes. Optionally invokes the callback immediately with the current value. Returns an unsubscribe function — call it to stop receiving updates. local unsubscribe = myState:Observe(function(value) print(value) end, true) -- true = also fire immediately with current value -- Later, to stop observing: unsubscribe()

searchDocs.title: Event Structure​
searchDocs.content: Events are accessed by key from the Events module. Each event entry should be a table with an Executor field:  -- Events/SixSeven.luau (example) local Trove = require(path.to.Trove) return { Executor = function(trove: Trove.Trove) -- This runs on every server when the event starts. -- Use the trove to manage cleanup. print("SixSeven event is active!") -- Example: double resource node output local AdminAbuse = require(script.Parent.Parent) AdminAbuse.EventMultipliers.ResourceNodeMultiplier:Set(1) trove:Add(function() AdminAbuse.EventMultipliers.ResourceNodeMultiplier:Set(0) end) end, }

searchDocs.title: The Executor Function​
searchDocs.content: The Executor receives a single argument — a Trove instance. Use it to register cleanup tasks:  The trove is cleaned automatically when the event expires or is replaced by another event.Any spawned threads, connections, or instances added to the trove will be destroyed on cleanup.You do not need to handle expiry manually.

searchDocs.title: GlobalEventKey​
searchDocs.content: The Events module exports a GlobalEventKey type which is a union of all valid event key strings. When you add a new event, add its key to this type so that AdminAbuse.event() gets type-safe autocompletion.

searchDocs.title: Triggering Events​
searchDocs.content: Once defined, trigger an event from any server script:  local AdminAbuse = require(path.to.AdminAbuse) AdminAbuse.event("SixSeven")   This will:  Persist the event timing in MemoryStoreServiceBroadcast it to all servers via MessagingServiceRun the Executor on every serverAutomatically clean up after the configured duration (default: 60 seconds)

searchDocs.title: Polling for Events​
searchDocs.content: By default, the Polling module watches for specific event keys in MemoryStoreService. To have an event discoverable via polling (for servers that miss the MessagingService broadcast), add its key to the pollForKeys array in Ping.luau:  task.defer(polling, { pollForKeys = { "SixSeven", "YourNewEvent", -- add new keys here }, })
